title: The Seventeenth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## September 19, 2021

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Seventeenth Sunday after Pentecost, falling within Ordinary Time in the liturgical calendar. The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this date are Proverbs 31:10-31 (the capable woman), Psalm 1, James 3:13-4:3-7 (wisdom from above), and Mark 9:30-37 (Jesus teaching about ambition and humility). The emphasis on wisdom literature and Jesus's countercultural teaching about greatness through service would have provided rich homiletic material for Pearson's sermon.

---

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was nine months into the leadership of Presiding Bishop Michael Curry's second term and navigating the ongoing pandemic's effects on worship and congregational life. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Kimberly Lucas (elected 2017), was in the process of reopening in-person worship at fuller capacity after Delta variant surges had caused some temporary restrictions in late summer 2021. No major national church controversies were reaching their peak at this specific moment, though tensions around vaccine mandates and return-to-normal protocols were emerging quietly across dioceses.

---

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced unseasonably warm weather that week with temperatures in the mid-80s and clear skies typical of early autumn. The Capitol Hill neighborhood and downtown area were seeing increased foot traffic as the Denver Public School system had just returned to full in-person learning (Denver schools opened August 23), and the Colorado Rockies were in late-season play (they would finish 2021 in fourth place in their division). Colorado's COVID-19 case counts were rising due to the Delta variant, though hospitalizations were stabilizing compared to summer peaks.

---

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Afghanistan withdrawal crisis dominated headlines as President Biden faced intense criticism over the chaotic evacuation completed just two weeks prior (August 31), with ongoing stories about American citizens and Afghan allies still stranded. Hurricane Ida had made landfall in Louisiana on August 29 as a catastrophic Category 4 storm and was still affecting the Gulf Coast and northeastern states with flooding in early September; the storm had caused significant loss of life and prompted national relief efforts. The U.S. Supreme Court had declined to block Texas's new six-week abortion ban (SB 8), which took effect September 1, setting off a major national controversy about Roe v. Wade that was intensifying across churches and denominations. September 18-19 saw significant protests across the country as thousands marched in support of vaccine mandates or against them, depending on location.

---

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Taliban had consolidated control of Afghanistan, hosting meetings with international delegations and raising questions about the future of the country; China condemned the U.S. evacuation as chaotic while Russia quietly engaged with Taliban representatives. The COVID-19 pandemic was killing record numbers globally, with India, Brazil, and European nations reporting significant surges; the World Health Organization was warning of new variants of concern. The UN General Assembly was convening in New York (started September 14), with numerous world leaders present and climate change emerging as a central theme for discussion.

---

CULTURAL MOMENT

American society was experiencing a fractured moment of reentry and uncertainty—businesses were reopening, schools were back in session, and yet anxiety about COVID-19, political divisions, and social polarization remained high. Trust in institutions was eroding visibly; many Americans were experiencing a kind of collective exhaustion and grief that hadn't yet been widely named as such. The Denver Broncos were about to begin their 2021 season, which would provide a rare unifying cultural touchstone for the local community. There was a palpable sense that "things should be back to normal by now," but they weren't, creating cognitive and emotional friction in many congregations.
